title: Extreme heat can be especially risky for people with dementia, experts warn - KOTA Territory News

full_text:
RAPID CITY, S.D. (KOTA) - The Alzheimer’s Foundation of America is urging caregivers to take extra precautions to protect people living with Alzheimer’s disease and other forms of dementia as dangerous heat continues across much of the country.
The organization released heat safety recommendations Wednesday, warning that dementia can affect a person’s ability to recognize thirst, communicate discomfort or realize they are overheating.
“Extreme heat is especially dangerous for someone with dementia because the illness can affect a person’s ability to recognize thirst, communicate discomfort, or realize they may be overheating,” said Jennifer Reeder, the foundation’s senior director of educational and social services.
The foundation recommends offering fluids frequently, limiting alcohol and caffeine, watching for signs of heat-related illness and ensuring people with dementia remain in cool environments.
Caregivers are also encouraged to monitor for wandering behavior, which can become more dangerous during extreme heat. The organization suggests providing safe indoor activities, keeping a recent photograph and medical information readily available, and having a plan in place if a loved one goes missing.
Warning signs of heat-related illness include excessive sweating, hot or red skin, muscle cramps, rapid pulse, headaches, dizziness, nausea and sudden changes in mental status. If symptoms develop, caregivers should move the person to a cooler location, apply cool compresses, encourage fluids if appropriate and call 911 if the person becomes unconscious, faints or experiences severe confusion.
The foundation also recommends identifying nearby cooling centers, preparing for power outages and arranging for friends, relatives or caregivers to check on loved ones during periods of extreme heat. Additional assistance is available through the Alzheimer’s Foundation of America’s dementia care helpline.
